message:
Add supervision narrative and enhance action tests

New features:
- supervision/narrative.rs: Process supervision narrative generation

Updates:
- renice.rs: Action improvements
- signature_cli.rs: CLI signature handling
- decision_real.rs: Real decision tests
- e2e_action_tray.rs: Enhanced E2E action tray tests
- Beads state updated

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"process_triage-aii.6.6","title":"Add real-system tests for cgroup/systemd detection","description":"## Objective
Validate cgroup and systemd detection against the live system where available (no mocks).

## Tasks
- On Linux, read `/proc/self/cgroup` and assert parsing produces stable v1/v2 structures.
- If systemd is available, call `systemctl show --property=... <pid>` and validate parsing.
- Validate cgroup-derived unit parsing fallback when systemctl is unavailable.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Tests pass on Linux with systemd and in containers (skip when not available).
- [ ] No mock outputs; use live system data.
- [ ] Clear skip messages for unsupported environments.

## Dependencies
- Blocks on: process_triage-aii.6.2 (real-process harness)
- Uses output from: process_triage-aii.6.1 (coverage map)

## Notes
- Must not assume systemd presence in CI; use capability checks.

 {"id":"process_triage-aii.6.7","title":"Real-system CLI E2E tests with detailed logging","description":"## Objective
Create CLI end-to-end integration tests that run against the real system (safe commands only) and emit detailed, structured logs.

## Scope
- `pt help`, `pt --version`, `pt history`, `pt scan` (non-destructive)
- Skip any action/kill flows unless running in a safe sandbox

## Tasks
- Extend BATS logging to write JSONL or timestamped logs per test.
- Add a dedicated `test/pt_e2e_real.bats` that:
  - uses isolated config paths
  - logs environment, command, status, and key output slices
  - records artifacts (stdout/stderr snapshots)
- Add a redaction check for any log output that could include sensitive data.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] E2E tests run without mocks and provide verbose logs.
- [ ] Tests are non-destructive and skip if environment is unsafe.
- [ ] Artifacts are easy to inspect when failures occur.

## Dependencies
- Blocks on: process_triage-aii.6.2 (real-process harness)
- Uses output from: process_triage-aii.6.1 (coverage map)

## Notes
- Should coordinate with existing E2E tasks in Testing Infrastructure epic.

 {"id":"process_triage-aii.6.8","title":"CI gating + artifacts for no-mock test track","description":"## Objective
Make the no-mock test track runnable in CI with clear gating and artifact collection.

## Tasks
- Add a CI job that runs the no-mock suite on Linux.
- Gate on capabilities (systemd presence, /proc access, network) and surface skips.
- Collect test logs as artifacts with per-test JSONL files.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] CI job runs the no-mock suite and publishes artifacts.
- [ ] Skipped tests are counted and reported.
- [ ] Failures provide enough logs for diagnosis.

## Dependencies
- Blocks on: process_triage-aii.6.7 (real-system E2E tests)
- Blocks on: process_triage-aii.6.3 / 6.4 / 6.5 / 6.6 (collector/tool/systemd tests)

## Notes
- Coordinate with existing CI test matrix tasks to avoid duplication.

 {"id":"process_triage-ed3.1","title":"Implement user signature customization (signatures.json + add/remove/list)","description":"## Context
Phase 11 (Pattern/Signature Library).

## Problem
Users need to teach pt about their own tools and workflows. A curated library is not enough; we need first-class user customization.

## Requirements
- Config file: `~/.config/process_triage/signatures.json`
- Commands:
  - `pt signature list`
  - `pt signature add` (guided, validates pattern)
  - `pt signature remove`
- Merge behavior:
  - built-in signatures + user signatures; user overrides allowed
- Validation:
  - schema validation
  - test match preview (`--dry-run` match against current processes)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] User signatures load and merge deterministically.
- [ ] CLI commands work and are documented.
- [ ] Invalid signatures fail with actionable errors.

## Test Plan
- Unit tests: merge/override behavior.
- E2E: create user signatures and confirm matches.

 {"id":"process_triage-ed3.2","title":"Implement signature-informed inference fast path (bypass/priors)","description":"## Context
Phase 11.

## Problem
Full inference can be expensive. When a process matches a high-confidence signature (e.g., known dev server pattern), we should be able to:
- skip deep inference
- or use signature-provided priors to accelerate and improve calibration

## Requirements
- Signature match returns a confidence score.
- If confidence ≥ threshold:
  - fast-path classification/prior override
  - still produce an evidence ledger (“matched signature X”)
- Track fast-path usage in telemetry.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] High-confidence matches trigger fast-path behavior.
- [ ] Output remains explainable and auditable.
- [ ] Fast-path can be disabled via config/policy.

## Test Plan
- Unit tests: match confidence thresholds.
- Golden tests: known signature fixtures.

 {"id":"process_triage-ed3.3","title":"Track and calibrate signature match confidence","description":"## Context
Phase 11.

## Problem
Signature confidence scores must be calibrated; otherwise we risk false certainty and unsafe recommendations.

## Approach
- Persist signature match outcomes:
  - match id, confidence score, eventual human decision/outcome
- Compute calibration metrics in shadow mode:
  - reliability curves for match confidence
  - confusion matrix by signature category
- Provide tooling to adjust thresholds and identify bad signatures.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Telemetry contains sufficient fields to analyze signature performance.
- [ ] Produces a calibration report artifact (DuckDB query or HTML section).

## Test Plan
- Unit tests: aggregation and metric computation on fixtures.

 {"id":"process_triage-erz","title":"Implement pt agent snapshot command","description":"## Overview
Implement the `pt agent snapshot` command that captures system state and creates a session for subsequent operations.

## From Plan Section 3.5

### Command Purpose
Returns a session_id and system state snapshot for subsequent agent operations. This is typically the first command in an agent workflow.

### Output Contract
```json
{
  'session_id': 'abc123',
  'host_id': 'devbox1',
  'timestamp': '2025-01-15T10:30:00Z',
  'system_state': {
    'load': [5.17, 4.89, 10.23],
    'cores': 64,
```

 {"id":"process_triage-ew5y","title":"Implement pt agent apply command","description":"**Purpose**: Implement the `pt agent apply` command that executes actions from a plan.

**Plan Document Reference**: Section 3.5.1 - Agent CLI Contract

**CLI Surface**:
```
pt agent apply --session <id> [OPTIONS]
```

**Required Options**:
- `--session <id>` - Session containing the plan to execute
- `--recommended` - Apply all recommended actions from the plan
- `--pids 123,456` - Apply to specific PIDs (must exist in session plan)
- `--targets 123:<start_id>,456:<start_id>` - Explicit identity tuples (preferred)
- `--yes` - Required for execution (explicit confirmation)
- `--resume` - Resume an interrupted session from where it left off

**Confidence-Bounded Automation** (`--robot` controls):
- `--min-posterior <threshold>` - Only act if posterior > threshold (e.g., 0.99)
- `--max-blast-radius <amount>` - Limit total impact per run (e.g., "2GB", "5 processes")
- `--max-kills <N>` - Limit number of kill actions per run
- `--require-known-signature` - Only act on pattern library matches
- `--only-categories <list>` - Only act on specified categories
- `--exclude-categories <list>` - Never act on specified categories
- `--abort-on-unknown` - Stop if any unexpected condition encountered

**Safety Requirements**:
- MUST revalidate process identity immediately before applying any action
- If `(pid,start_id,uid)` mismatches, MUST block and require fresh plan
- MUST respect `--shadow` and `--dry-run` modes (no actual actions)
- MUST acquire per-user "pt lock" before executing
- MUST support supervisor-aware actions when supervisor detected

**Resumability**:
- Sessions track: plan, applied actions, pending actions, outcomes
- `--resume` continues from where interrupted
- Idempotent: re-running a completed action is a no-op

**Exit Codes**:
- 2: Actions executed successfully
- 3: Partial failure executing actions
- 4: Blocked by safety gates / policy
- 6: Session interrupted / resumable

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the described scope with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).
- [ ] Includes unit/integration/E2E coverage for the highest-risk paths.

Implement the progress event system that reports collection stages to TUI and agent CLI.

- scan_started, scan_progress, scan_complete

}

- Support multiple subscribers (TUI, file, network)
- Events should be cheap (not slow down collection)
- Include timing information for performance analysis

- deep_scan_{started,progress,complete}
- inference_{started,progress,complete}
- decision_{started,complete}

 {"id":"process_triage-ica.2","title":"EPIC: Interactive Documentation and Tutorials","description":"## Overview
Create comprehensive documentation with interactive tutorials, video references, and example configurations for common setups.

## Background
Current documentation (README, AGENTS.md) is good but lacks interactive learning materials. Modern CLI tools benefit from rustlings-style tutorials and video walkthroughs.

- Interactive CLI tutorials (rustlings pattern) accelerate learning

- Example configurations reduce time-to-value
- Architecture docs help contributors

### 1. Interactive Tutorial (\`pt learn\`)

- Topics: 
  1. Basic scan (scanning first process list)
  2. Reading evidence (understanding the ledger)
  3. Understanding recommendations (classification scores)
  4. Applying plans (safe kill workflow)
  5. Robot mode basics (automation introduction)
  6. Shadow mode (calibration and learning)
  7. Deep scan (advanced evidence)
- Progress tracking (local state)
- Built into binary (no external dependency)

### 2. Example Configurations
- \`examples/configs/developer.json\`: Aggressive for dev machines
  - Lower thresholds, more frequent scans
  - Test runner patterns pre-configured
- \`examples/configs/server.json\`: Conservative for production
  - Higher thresholds, longer cooldowns
  - Database protection patterns
- \`examples/configs/ci.json\`: Headless CI/CD mode
  - No prompts, JSON output

  - ADR-003: Safety gate design
  - ADR-004: FDR control mechanism
  - ADR-005: Telemetry lake architecture
- Component diagrams (Mermaid in docs)
- Data flow documentation
- Security model documentation

### 4. Video References
- README links to video walkthroughs
- Asciinema recordings for quick demos:

  - Plan review and apply (60s)
  - Robot mode for agents (45s)
- YouTube playlist for longer tutorials

### 5. API Reference
- Generated from doc comments (cargo doc)
- Hosted on docs.rs
- JSON schema documentation with examples
- Exit code reference table
- Error message catalog

 {"id":"process_triage-im2a","title":"Implement prior tuning from calibration data","description":"## Task: Prior Tuning from Calibration Data (Phase 9.3)

### Description
Automatically adjust prior distributions based on calibration data to improve model accuracy.

### Requirements
1. **Prior Parameters to Tune**
   - Process type priors (Beta α, β for each type)
   - Evidence weights (coefficient for each evidence source)
   - Threshold boundaries (KILL/REVIEW/SPARE cutoffs)
   - Expected lifetimes per process type

2. **Tuning Algorithm**
   ```
   For each prior parameter θ:
     1. Compute gradient of log-likelihood w.r.t. θ
     2. Update: θ_new = θ_old + η × ∇log(L)
     3. Regularize to prevent overfitting: θ_new = λ × θ_prior + (1-λ) × θ_new
     4. Validate on holdout set
     5. Accept if validation loss improves
   ```

3. **Tuning Output**
   ```json
   {
     "priors": {
       "test_runner": {"alpha": 3.2, "beta": 4.1, "expected_lifetime_hours": 0.75},
       "dev_server": {"alpha": 2.1, "beta": 5.8, "expected_lifetime_hours": 12},
       "agent_shell": {"alpha": 2.5, "beta": 6.2, "expected_lifetime_hours": 6}
     },
     "evidence_weights": {
       "orphaned": 38,
       "zero_cpu": 22,
       "high_memory": 12,
       "age_ratio": 1.8
     },
     "thresholds": {
       "kill": 62,
       "review": 28
     },
     "validation_metrics": {
       "brier_before": 0.15,
       "brier_after": 0.11,
       "improvement": "27%"
     }
   }
   ```

4. **Safety Constraints**
   - Maximum change per tuning round: ±20%
   - Minimum KILL threshold: 50
   - Maximum SPARE threshold: 40
   - Require N≥100 observations per type before tuning

### CLI Interface
```bash
# Preview tuning changes without applying
pt calibrate tune --dry-run

# Apply tuning with backup
pt calibrate tune --apply --backup

# Reset to default priors
pt calibrate reset
```

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Tuning improves Brier score on validation set
- [ ] Safety constraints prevent extreme adjustments
- [ ] Tuning is reversible with backup
- [ ] Changes are logged for audit","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T09:03:43.146474378Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-15T09:03:43.146474378Z","compaction_level":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-im2a","depends_on_id":"process_triage-21f","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-15T11:49:59.826466521Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"process_triage-imji","title":"Implement visual session diff (side-by-side comparison)","description":"## Overview
Implement visual side-by-side comparison of two sessions, highlighting differences in candidates, classifications, and outcomes.

## Background
Users need to understand how their system changed between scans, or compare different configurations. Visual diff makes this easy.

## Scope

### 1. Diff Command
- \`pt diff <session1> <session2>\`: Compare two sessions
- \`pt diff --baseline\`: Compare current vs baseline
- \`pt diff --last\`: Compare current vs last session
- Output: Side-by-side view of differences

### 2. Diff Categories
**Processes:**
- New processes (in session2 but not session1)
- Gone processes (in session1 but not session2)
- Changed classification (same PID, different class)
- Changed score (significant score delta)

**Outcomes:**
- Killed in both / neither
- Killed in one only
- Classification changed but same outcome

**Evidence:**
- Evidence score changes
- New evidence sources
- Missing evidence sources

### 3. Output Formats
- **TUI**: Side-by-side panes with highlighting
- **JSON**: Structured diff for agents
- **Plain**: Text-based diff for terminals

### 4. Filtering
- \`--changed-only\`: Only show differences
- \`--category <type>\`: Filter by change type
- \`--min-score-delta N\`: Only show significant score changes

---

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests
- **File**: \`crates/pt-core/src/session/diff_test.rs\`
- **Coverage target**: 90% for diff algorithm
- Test cases:
  - Process matching by PID/identity
  - New/gone process detection
  - Classification change detection
  - Score delta calculation
  - Evidence diff generation
  - Empty session handling

### Integration Tests
- **File**: \`crates/pt-core/tests/diff_integration.rs\`
- Test cases:
  - Diff of real session snapshots
  - Diff formatting for all output types
  - Filter application works
  - Large session diff performance

### E2E Tests
- **File**: \`test/diff_e2e.bats\`
- Test scenarios:
  - Record session1, change system, record session2, diff shows changes
  - \`pt diff --baseline\` works after baseline set
  - \`pt diff --last\` compares to previous
  - JSON output validates against schema
  - TUI diff displays correctly (visual check)
- **Artifact logging**: Diff outputs, session snapshots

### Golden File Tests
- Pre-recorded sessions with known differences
- Compare diff output to expected
- Detect regressions in diff algorithm

---

Implement the `pt agent inbox` command for viewing pending plans from dormant mode escalations.

## From Plan Sections 3.5, 3.7

### Command Purpose
View 'plans ready for review' generated by dormant mode daemon or other automated triggers. Provides a notification queue for agents.

### Usage
```
pt agent inbox                    # List pending items
pt agent inbox --format json      # JSON output
pt agent inbox --ack <item_id>    # Acknowledge/dismiss item
pt agent inbox --clear            # Clear all acknowledged items
```

### Output Contract
```json

  'items': [
    {
      'id': 'inbox-001',

      'created_at': '2025-01-15T10:00:00Z',

      'deferred_session_id': 'def456',
      'acknowledged': false

}
```

 {"id":"process_triage-isy","title":"Implement shadow mode model validation framework","description":"## Overview
Build a framework for validating model predictions against ground truth outcomes.

## Background
Shadow mode observes without acting, creating an opportunity to validate predictions. When a process eventually exits (user killed it, it completed, crash, etc.), we can check whether our prediction matched reality.

## Why It Matters
Model validation is essential for improving accuracy over time. By tracking predictions vs outcomes, we can identify systematic biases, tune priors, and measure overall model quality. This closes the feedback loop for continuous improvement.

## Technical Approach
1. Track predictions for each process at each observation
2. Capture ground truth when process terminates
3. Compute calibration metrics
4. Generate reports for analysis
5. Feed back to prior tuning

## Ground Truth Sources
- Process exit: exit code, signal, timing
- User action: pt kill (confirmed prediction) or pt spare (rejected)
- Natural completion: Normal exit after expected lifetime
- Crash: Non-zero exit, core dump
- System shutdown: SIGTERM from init during shutdown

## Metrics Computed
- Calibration: Are 80% confidence predictions correct 80% of time?
- Discrimination: AUC for abandoned vs useful classification
- Brier score: Mean squared prediction error
- Expected vs actual loss: Did our recommendations minimize loss?

## Validation Reports
- Daily calibration plot
- Per-category accuracy (test runners, dev servers, etc.)
- Most common false positives and negatives
- Prior adjustment recommendations

## Feedback Loop
Validation results can automatically adjust:
- Category-specific priors (signatures)
- Evidence weights (likelihood ratios)
- Threshold calibration (decision boundaries)

## Success Criteria
- All process terminations captured with ground truth
- Calibration metrics computed accurately
- Reports generated automatically
- Prior tuning recommendations actionable

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

 {"id":"process_triage-sj6.1","title":"Implement zombie and D-state handling in action planning/execution","description":"## Purpose
Implement correct **zombie (`Z`)** and **uninterruptible sleep (`D`)** handling in action planning and execution.

These two OS states are special because “just kill it” is either impossible (zombie) or often ineffective/dangerous (D-state).

## Definitions (so future self doesn’t forget)
- **Zombie (`Z`)**: the process is already dead; it cannot be killed. The fix is to get the *parent* to reap it (or restart/kill the parent/supervisor).
- **Uninterruptible sleep (`D`)**: usually waiting on kernel I/O; may ignore SIGKILL. Blind killing can hang workflows and create false confidence.

## Required Behavior
### Zombie (`Z`)
- Detect zombies reliably.
- Do **not** propose `SIGTERM`/`SIGKILL` as the primary action for the zombie PID.
- Propose a safe alternative action plan, e.g.:
  - identify parent process
  - recommend supervisor-level action if parent is supervised
  - as a last resort, propose killing/restarting the parent (policy-gated)
- Ensure explainability:
  - evidence ledger / action rationale must clearly state “cannot kill a zombie; must address parent.”

### D-state (`D`)
- Detect D-state reliably.
- Default recommendation should be **investigate/mitigate**, not “kill harder.”
- Surface diagnostics (as evidence/provenance), e.g.:
  - `wchan` (what it’s blocked on)
  - relevant I/O counters deltas
- If any destructive action is proposed, it must be:
  - explicitly policy-gated
  - explained as low-probability-of-success
  - accompanied by suggested next steps (restart supervisor, check mount, etc.)

## Interaction With Decision Theory
- Expected-loss computation must treat Z/D specially:
  - Z: action feasibility constraint (kill action invalid)
  - D: action success probability low; increased uncertainty/robust gating

## Logging / Telemetry Requirements
- Always log:
  - detected state (`Z`/`D`)
  - chosen action routing (parent/supervisor/investigate)
  - diagnostics gathered (wchan, etc.)
- Ensure logs are useful for postmortems (why we didn’t kill / why we suggested restart).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Zombie targets never receive a direct kill action in the generated plan.
- [ ] Zombie handling routes to a parent/supervisor-aware action (or explicit “investigate only”) with clear rationale.
- [ ] D-state targets default to investigate/mitigate and surface wchan/diagnostics.
- [ ] Any kill-like action for D-state is policy-gated and clearly labeled “low confidence / may not work.”
- [ ] Structured logs/telemetry include state classification and routing decisions.

## Test Plan
- Unit:
  - planner routing logic for Z vs non-Z
  - planner routing logic for D vs non-D
- Integration (paired with safety test bead `process_triage-c982`):
  - fixture processes (or mocked proc table) representing Z and D states
  - assert action plan contents + exit codes + rationale text
- Logging:
  - tests capture logs and assert required fields (state, routing, diagnostics).

## Dependencies
- Deep scan/proc state visibility: `process_triage-cki`
- Policy/safety infrastructure: `process_triage-dvi`
- Action execution primitives: `process_triage-sj6.3`
- Identity/target contract: `process_triage-o8m`

 {"id":"process_triage-sj6.2","title":"Implement pause/resume actions (SIGSTOP/SIGCONT) with group-aware targeting","description":"## Overview
Implement the **pause** and **resume** actions from Plan §6:
- pause: SIGSTOP (PID or process group)
- resume: SIGCONT

These actions are core to the staged execution protocol: pause/observe is often safer than kill.

## Key Plan Constraints
- Pause is reversible but not free: can stall services, hold locks, and block dependent processes.
- Pause must be gated behind blast-radius checks and session-protection checks.

## Requirements
### 1) Targeting semantics
- Support targeting:
  - single PID
  - process group (PGID) when children exist or when PID is part of a job/workload tree
- Prefer group-aware operations when child processes exist (avoid leaving orphans).

### 2) Safety gates (must be enforced)
- Identity revalidation immediately before signaling: at minimum `(pid, start_id, uid)`.
- Default same-UID restriction.
- Session safety: protect the active user session chain (shell, tmux/screen, ssh) by default.
- Blast radius / dependency gate: do not pause high-impact processes unless explicitly confirmed (and never in robot mode unless policy allows).

### 3) Verification
- After pause: verify state reflects stop (best-effort; OS-specific).
- After resume: verify the process can run again.
- Record outcomes and timing to telemetry.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Pause/resume work for PID targets.
- [ ] Pause/resume work for process-group targets.
- [ ] All safety gates are enforced and logged.
- [ ] Post-action verification and telemetry are recorded.

## Test Plan
- Integration: spawn a controlled CPU burner process + child worker; pause group and verify CPU drops; resume and verify recovery.
- Safety: ensure session-protection blocks pausing the current shell/tmux.
- E2E: staged plan includes pause step and reports after-action diff.
- Logging: verify structured action outcome records include durations and verification results.

 {"id":"process_triage-sj6.3","title":"Implement kill actions (SIGTERM→SIGKILL) with PID+process-group support and TOCTOU safety","description":"## Overview
Implement the **kill** action from Plan §6:
- staged signals: SIGTERM with grace period → SIGKILL as last resort
- target types: PID or process group (PGID)

This is the most destructive action and must be conservative by default.

## Requirements
### 1) Targeting semantics
- If the target has children (or is a known workload tree), prefer group-aware kill (PGID) to avoid leaving orphans.
- Support kill-tree semantics where available (but always derived from verified identity, not stale PID lists).

### 2) Safety gates (non-negotiable)
- Identity revalidation immediately before sending any signal:
  - at minimum `(pid, start_id, uid)`; include `boot_id` where possible.
- Default same-UID enforcement; cross-UID kill requires explicit policy + privileges.
- Protected process denylist must hard-block.
- Data-loss gate integration: open write handles inflate loss and can hard-block robot mode.
- D-state and zombie handling:
  - zombies cannot be killed directly; route to parent remediation.
  - D-state processes may not respond; prefer investigation; avoid repeated SIGKILL loops.

### 3) Verification + outcomes
- Verify termination outcome (process absent OR identity mismatch indicates PID reuse) and record results.
- Detect respawn (supervisor) and emit `supervisor_conflict` failures.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] SIGTERM then SIGKILL escalation works for PID targets.
- [ ] Group-aware kill works and avoids leaving orphans.
- [ ] Identity revalidation blocks PID-reuse races deterministically.
- [ ] Outcomes/respawn detection are recorded in telemetry.

## Test Plan
- Integration: spawn a parent+child tree; kill via PGID and verify all exit.
- Safety: simulate PID reuse via mocked snapshots and ensure kill is blocked.
- Supervisor: mock respawn loop and ensure failure_type is `supervisor_conflict`.
- Logging: verify full audit trail for signals, wait times, and verification results.

Implement the **renice** action from Plan §6: adjust process scheduling priority as a reversible mitigation step when kill is too risky.

## Requirements
### 1) Action semantics
- Support renice for:
  - PID
  - process group (optional; if supported)
- Record the previous nice value and the new nice value (reversibility).

### 2) Safety + privilege
- Enforce same-UID by default.
- Handle permission failures cleanly (`permission_denied`) and attach failure recovery tree.
- Policy must bound how aggressive renice can be (e.g., do not allow negative nice without explicit privilege + allowlist).

### 3) Verification
- Verify the nice value changed (best-effort per platform) and log the outcome.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Renice action can be applied and verified for same-UID processes.
- [ ] Permission denied produces a structured failure type and recovery hint.
- [ ] Prior nice value is captured for reversibility.

## Test Plan
- Integration: spawn a CPU burner, renice it, verify scheduling priority changes.
- Negative tests: attempt to renice without privilege and verify `permission_denied` handling.
- Logging: ensure before/after nice values appear in action outcomes.

 {"id":"process_triage-sj6.5","title":"Implement cgroup freeze/quarantine action (cgroup v2 freezer) with fallbacks","description":"## Overview
Implement the **cgroup freeze/quarantine** action from Plan §6:
- Use cgroup v2 freezer when available (or the closest equivalent).
- Intended as a reversible containment action that is often less disruptive than SIGSTOP for complex workloads.

## Requirements
### 1) Capability detection
- Detect whether cgroup v2 freezer is available and writable.
- If unavailable, downgrade to safer alternatives per policy (e.g., CPU throttle or renice) and record the downgrade.

### 2) Action semantics
- Identify the process’s effective cgroup.
- Apply freeze to the appropriate scope (PID’s cgroup or a quarantine subgroup) without freezing unrelated processes.
- Capture reversibility metadata (unfreeze instructions).

### 3) Safety
- Enforce same-UID by default.
- Gate behind blast-radius checks and protected-process checks.
- Avoid freezing core system services; prefer supervisor-aware stop/restart for supervised services.

### 4) Verification
- Verify frozen state where possible (best-effort; cgroup state indicators).
- Log outcomes and duration.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Freezer action works on cgroup v2 systems where permitted.
- [ ] Fallback path is deterministic when freezer is unavailable.
- [ ] Freezing does not accidentally capture unrelated workloads.

## Test Plan
- Integration: in a cgroup v2-capable test environment, create a test cgroup, place a PID, freeze/unfreeze and verify behavior.
- Negative tests: permission denied and missing freezer controller.
- Logging: ensure downgrade and unfreeze hints are recorded.

 {"id":"process_triage-sj6.6","title":"Implement cgroup CPU throttle action (cgroup v2 cpu.max) with safety gates","description":"## Overview
Implement the **cgroup CPU throttle** action from Plan §6:
- cgroup v2: adjust `cpu.max` (quota/period)
- cgroup v1 (optional): `cpu.cfs_quota_us` / `cpu.cfs_period_us`

This is a reversible mitigation action intended to reduce load while preserving process state.

## Requirements
### 1) Capability detection
- Detect cgroup version and whether CPU controller settings are writable.
- If not supported, downgrade deterministically (renice/pause) per policy.

### 2) Action semantics
- Apply throttling to an appropriate scope so only the target workload is constrained.
- Capture previous settings for reversibility.
- Policy must bound throttle aggressiveness.

### 3) Safety
- Same-UID by default.
- Protected process hard-block.
- Explainability: ledger must show why throttle was chosen and the expected relief.

### 4) Verification
- Verify throttle settings changed (read-back) and record outcome.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] CPU throttle works on supported systems.
- [ ] Reversal metadata is captured.
- [ ] Fallback behavior is deterministic and logged.

## Test Plan
- Integration: throttle a known CPU burner in an isolated cgroup and confirm CPU usage drops.
- Negative tests: permission denied/unavailable controller.
- Logging: ensure before/after cpu.max values appear in outcomes.

 {"id":"process_triage-sj6.7","title":"Implement cpuset quarantine action (cpuset controller)","description":"## Overview
Implement the **cpuset quarantine** action from Plan §6:
- constrain a workload to a limited CPU set (and possibly NUMA nodes) to reduce system-wide impact.

This is an advanced containment action that must be conservative and opt-in.

## Requirements
### 1) Capability detection
- Detect cpuset controller availability (cgroup v2 cpuset or v1 cpuset).
- Verify write permissions.

### 2) Action semantics
- Create/select a quarantine cpuset and move the target process group/workload into it.
- Capture previous cpuset membership for reversibility.

### 3) Safety gates
- Protected denylist hard-block.
- Ensure quarantine does not starve the workload in a way that causes deadlocks (policy min CPU count).
- Never apply by default in robot mode unless explicitly allowed.

### 4) Verification
- Verify cpuset assignment changed and log outcomes.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Cpuset quarantine works when supported/allowed.
- [ ] Reversal metadata is captured.
- [ ] Safety gates prevent unsafe cpuset assignments.

## Test Plan
- Integration: in a cpuset-capable test environment, quarantine a CPU burner and verify affinity changes.
- Negative: controller missing/permission denied.
- Logging: verify before/after cpuset assignments captured.
